summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how Beagle Bros software from his youth taught him that the best programs prioritize fun over features. He argues that interactive, animated learning experiences are far superior to passive reading, and that the vision of 'interactive textbooks' dreamed up in 1985 is now achievable through modern web technologies. The post catalogs numerous examples of interactive web-based educational content that embody this philosophy, from algorithm visualizations to playable programming tutorials. Atwood concludes that he wants future generations to learn from programs that invite experimentation rather than passive consumption.
key_points: The best way to create fun programs is to have fun writing them in the first place
Interactive, dynamic programs teach better than passive reading of textbooks
Modern web technologies (JavaScript, HTML5) can now deliver the interactive textbook experience that was only a dream in 1985
Learning should involve experimentation and play, not just reading
Platform-specific native apps are less desirable than web-based solutions for educational content
money_quotes: Instead of guaranteeing fancy features or compatibility or error free operation, Beagle Bros software promised something else altogether: fun.
Apparently, the best way to create fun programs for users is to make sure you had fun writing them in the first place.
They taught me how much more fun it was to learn by playing with an interactive, dynamic program instead of passively reading about concepts in a book.
One day, all books will be interactive and animated.
Imagine a world where textbooks didn't just present a wall of text to you, the learner, but actually engaged you, played with you, and invited experimentation. Right there on the page.
C'mon. Type something. See what happens.
I want them to learn, as I did, that our programs are fun to use.
key_insight: The future of education lies in interactive programs that invite experimentation rather than passive reading, and modern web technology has finally made the 1985 dream of animated, playable textbooks a reality.
